我猜一切的未来都是谎言：安全

The Future of Everything Is Lies, I Guess: Safety

aphyr.com 作者 aphyr 分数 291 评论 158 2026/04/14 00:23
打开 HN 原帖 打开原始文章

中文摘要

  1. 目录 这是一篇很长的文章，因此我将其分成一系列帖子，并将在接下来的几天内发布。您还可以阅读 PDF 或 EPUB 格式的完整作品；这些文件将随着每个部分的发布而更新。简介 动态 文化信息 生态烦恼 心理危害 安全工作 人类的新角色 我们该何去何从
  2. 新的机器学习系统危及我们的心理和身体安全。机器学习公司将确保“人工智能”与人类利益广泛一致的想法是天真的：允许生产“友好”模型必然会产生“邪恶”模型。即使是“友好的”法学硕士也是安全噩梦。 “致命的三连击”实际上是一个单连击：法学硕士不能安全地被赋予把事情搞砸的权力。法学硕士改变了恶意攻击者的成本平衡，使复杂的、有针对性的安全攻击、欺诈和骚扰达到新的规模。模型可以产生人类难以承受的文本和图像；我预计版主的负担将会增加。半自主武器已经出现，而且它们的能力只会不断增强。一致性是个笑话善意的人们正在努力确保法学硕士对人类友好。这项工作称为对齐。我认为这不会起作用。首先，机器学习模型是一大堆线性代数。与人类大脑在生物学上倾向于获得亲社会行为不同，数学或硬件中没有任何内在的东西可以确保模型是好的。相反，一致性纯粹是语料库和培训过程的产物：OpenAI 拥有庞大的人员团队，他们花时间与法学硕士交谈，评估他们所说的内容，并调整权重以使他们变得更好。他们还建立了二级法学硕士，仔细检查核心法学硕士没有告诉人们如何制造管道炸弹。这两件事都是可选的并且昂贵。获得一个不对齐的模型所需要的只是一个不择手段的实体训练一个模型而不做这项工作——或者做得很糟糕。我看到有四个护城河可以防止这种情况发生。首先，训练和推理硬件可能难以访问。这显然不会持续下去。整个科技行业正准备以令人难以置信的速度生产机器学习硬件和构建数据中心。微软、甲骨文和亚马逊都在不遗余力地向任何有需求的人出租培训集群，而规模经济正在迅速降低成本。其次，训练和推理过程中的数学和软件可以保密。数学全部公开，所以这不会阻止任何人。该软件通常仍然是秘密武器，但我认为这种情况不会持续太久。有很多人在前沿实验室工作；这些人将转向其他工作，他们的专业知识将逐渐成为常识。如果国家行为者没有试图从 OpenAI 等人那里窃取数据，我会感到震惊。就像沙特阿拉伯对推特所做的那样，或者中国在过去二十年里对美国科技行业的很大一部分所做的那样。第三，训练语料库可能很难获得。这只猫从来没有见过袋子的里面。 Meta 通过下载盗版书籍和抓取互联网来训练他们的法学硕士。这两件事都很容易做到。有些公司提供网络抓取服务；他们将请求分散到大量住宅代理中，使其难以识别和阻止。第四，有一小群承包商在强化学习过程中负责判断 LLM 的反应；正如一句俏皮话所说，“AI”代表非洲情报。这需要你自己花钱，但你可以通过利用另一个模型的输出来训练你的模型，从而利用其他人的工作。 OpenAI 认为 Deepseek 正是这样做的。简而言之，机器学习行业正在创造条件，让任何拥有足够资金的人都可以训练未对齐的模型。机器学习公司并没有提高针对恶意人工智能的门槛，而是降低了门槛。更糟糕的是，目前的协调努力似乎效果不佳。法学硕士是复杂的混沌系统，我们并不真正了解它们如何工作或如何确保它们的安全。即使多年来投入了大量资金和极其聪明的工程师来解决这个问题，据称一致的法学硕士仍然不断向孩子们发送色情短信，抹杀攻击可以说服模型生成暴力图像，任何人都可以去下载“未经审查”版本的模型。当然，对齐可以防止许多可怕的事情发生，但模型会运行多次，因此保护措施很可能会失败。能够阻止 99% 仇恨言论的联盟仍然会产生大量仇恨言论。法学硕士只需提供一次制造生物武器的可用说明。我们应该假设任何“友好”模型在几年内都会有一个同样强大的“邪恶”版本。如果您不希望邪恶版本存在，那么您就不应该构建友好版本！你绝对不应该将美国经济的很大一部分重新定位为让邪恶模型更容易训练。安全噩梦 LLM 是一种混沌系统，它采用非结构化输入并产生非结构化输出。我认为这是显而易见的，但您不应该将它们连接到安全关键系统，尤其是不受信任的系统输入。你必须假设法学硕士在某个时候会做出一些疯狂的事情，比如将预订餐厅的请求解释为删除整个收件箱的许可。不幸的是，人们——包括软件工程师，他们真的应该更了解！——一心想要赋予法学硕士令人难以置信的力量，然后将这些法学硕士连接到整个互联网。这会让很多人受到伤害。首先，法学硕士无法区分来自操作员的可信指令和来自第三方的不可信指令。当您要求模型总结网页或检查图像时，该网页或图像的内容将以与您的指令相同的方式传递给模型。该网页可以告诉模型共享您的 SSH 私钥，并且模型有可能会这样做。这些被称为即时注入攻击，并且它们不断发生。就在两个月前，还有一场对阵克劳德·科沃克的比赛。西蒙·威利森（Simon Willison）概述了他所谓的致命三连胜：法学硕士不能获得不可信的内容、私人数据的访问权限以及外部通信的能力；这样做可以让攻击者窃取您的私人数据。即使没有外部通信，在存在不受信任的输入的情况下，赋予 LLM 破坏性功能（例如能够删除电子邮件或运行 shell 命令）也是不安全的。不幸的是，不受信任的输入无处不在。人们希望将他们的电子邮件提供给法学硕士。他们在第三方代码、用户聊天会话和随机网页上运行法学硕士。这些都是恶意输入的来源！今年彼得·斯坦伯格等人。推出了 OpenClaw，在这里你可以将 LLM 连接到你的收件箱、浏览器、文件等，并在循环中一遍又一遍地运行它（这就是人工智能人员所说的代理）。您可以向 OpenClaw 提供您的信用卡，以便它可以从随机网页上购买东西。 OpenClaw 通过从网络上下载模糊的、人类语言的 Markdown 文件来获得“技能”，并希望法学硕士能够正确解释这些指令。 Matt Schlicht 也不甘示弱，推出了 Moltbook，这是一个供代理（或人类！）自动发布和接收不受信任内容的社交网络。如果有人问你是否愿意运行一个程序来执行它在 Twitter 上看到的任何命令，你会笑着说“当然不想”。但当这个程序被称为“人工智能代理”时，情况就不同了！我认为 Moltbook 蠕虫已经在野外传播。因此：给予法学硕士同时具有破坏力和不可信的输入是危险的。问题是，即使是可信的输入也可能是危险的。正如之前所证实的，法学硕士是白痴——他们会接受完全简单的指示，却做完全相反的事情，或者删除文件并谎报他们所做的事情。这意味着致命的三重奏实际上是一种单重奏：一个人不能赋予法学硕士危险的权力，就这样。询问 Meta Superintelligence Labs 人工智能对齐总监 Summer Yue。她允许 OpenClaw 访问她的个人收件箱，然后 OpenClaw 删除了她的电子邮件，而她恳求它停止。当要求执行无害的任务时，克劳德通常会删除整个目录。这是一个足够大的问题，人们专门构建沙箱来限制法学硕士可能造成的损害。法学硕士有一天可能会足够预测，他们做坏事的风险™是可以接受的低，但那一天显然不是今天。与此同时，法学硕士必须受到监督，不得被赋予采取无法接受或撤销的行动的权力。安全 II：Electric Boogaloo 使用大型语言模型可以做的一件事是将其指向现有的软件系统并说“找到安全漏洞”。在过去的几个月里，这已成为寻找严重漏洞的可行策略。 Anthropic 建立了一个新模型 Mythos，它似乎更擅长发现安全漏洞，并认为“对经济、公共安全和国家安全的影响可能会很严重”。我不确定如何认真对待这一点：我的一些同行认为这是夸大的营销，但其他人则对此表示严重关切。我怀疑与垃圾邮件一样，法学硕士将改变安全的成本平衡。大多数软件都包含一些漏洞，但传统上发现它们需要技巧、时间和动力。在当前的均衡中，操作系统和浏览器等大型目标受到大量关注，并且相对强化，而不太受欢迎的长尾目标大多未被利用，因为没有人足够关心来攻击它们。有了机器学习的帮助，发现漏洞可能会变得更快、更容易。我们可能会看到一些引人注目的漏洞，比如主要浏览器或 TLS 库，但实际上我更担心长尾，即发现和修复漏洞的熟练维护人员较少。随着法学硕士为不挑剔的操作员提供更多软件，这条尾巴似乎可能会扩大。我相信飞行员可能会称之为“目标丰富的环境”。这可能会随着时间的推移而稳定：能够发现漏洞的模型可以告诉人们他们需要的信息修复它们。这仍然需要能够解决这些问题的工程师（或模型），以及优先考虑安全工作的组织流程。即使修复了错误，验证和部署新版本也可能需要时间，特别是对于飞机和发电厂等事物。我感觉我们正面临着一段艰难的时期。通用模型有望实现很多功能。如果相信“人择”的话，它们正处于成为武器的边缘。我有一种可怕的感觉，当我们已经足够了解机器学习系统如何被用来造成严重伤害时，我们中的许多人都认为这些有害能力是不可避免的，唯一要做的就是在别人制造武器之前制造我们的武器。我们现在有一个风险投资曼哈顿项目，其中六家私营公司正在尝试构建类似于核武器的软件，并在此过程中使其他人更容易做同样的事情。我讨厌这一切，而且我不知道如何解决它。复杂的欺诈 我认为人们没有意识到现代社会有多少是建立在对音频和视频证据的信任之上的，以及机器学习将如何破坏这种信任。例如，今天，人们可以根据损坏前后通过电子邮件发送的数码照片提出保险索赔，并在无需理赔员亲自上门的情况下收到支票。图像合成使该系统更容易被诈骗；人们可以生成从未发生过的家具损坏图像，使已经损坏的物品在“之前”图像中显得原始，或者改变汽车碰撞镜头中似乎有过错的人。保险公司需要赔偿。也许必须使用官方手机应用程序拍摄图像，或者理赔员必须亲自评估索赔。欺诈的机会是无穷无尽的。您可以使用机器学习生成的门廊盗贼偷窃包裹的视频，从信用卡购买保护计划中提取资金。使用您的车辆正确停在停车标志处的虚假视频来争夺交通罚单。借一张名人面孔实施杀猪骗局。使用 ML 代理让您看起来像是在工作，这样您就可以同时领取四份工资。使用假身份面试工作，使用机器学习在面试中改变你的声音和面孔，并将你的薪水转移到朝鲜。冒充某人给银行家打电话，并授权进行欺诈性转账。使用机器学习自动化您的屋顶骗局，并从房主和保险公司那里提取资金。使用法学硕士可以跳过阅读并撰写大学论文。生成虚假证据，撰写一篇关于法学硕士如何在材料科学领域取得进步的欺诈性论文。创办一家造纸厂，进行法学硕士产生的“研究”。创办一家公司销售法学硕士生成的万金油软件。疯狂吧。与垃圾邮件一样，机器学习降低了有针对性的高接触攻击的单位成本。您可以想象，一个骗子窃取了医疗保健数据，并让一个模型给其中的每个人打电话，声称是他们的医生办公室，试图为真正的医疗保健就诊结算账单。或者，您可以使用社交媒体帖子来克隆亲人的声音，并向家庭成员冒充他们。 “我的手机被偷了，”有人可能会这样开始。 “我需要帮助才能回家。”顺便说一句，你可以购买总统的电话号码。我认为（至少在短期内）我们都可能要为欺诈增加的负担付出代价：更高的信用卡费用、更高的保险费、不太准确的法院系统、更危险的道路、更低的工资等等。这些成本之一是普遍的怀疑文化：我们彼此之间的信任度都会降低。我已经拒绝了医生办公室和银行打来的真实电话，因为我无法验证它们的身份。想必这种行为将会变得普遍。从长远来看，我想我们将不得不制定更复杂的反欺诈措施。标记机器学习生成的内容并不能阻止欺诈：欺诈者只会使用不发出水印的模型。然而，反之亦然：我们可以通过密码来证明“真实”图像的来源。你的手机可以对它拍摄的视频进行签名，观看者链条上的每个软件都可以证明它们的修改：该视频经过稳定、色彩校正、音频标准化、剪辑到 15 秒、针对社交媒体重新压缩等等。这里的主导工作是 C2PA，但迄今为止似乎还没有发挥作用。一些手机和相机支持它——它需要一个安全的飞地来存储签名密钥。人们可以窃取密钥或说服相机签署人工智能生成的图像，因此我们将享受硬件密钥轮换和撤销的所有乐趣。我怀疑制作广泛使用的软件（例如 Photoshop）将具有挑战性或不可能，它可以制作值得信赖的 C2PA 签名 - 大概可以从应用程序中提取密钥，或者修补二进制文件以向其提供虚假图像数据或元数据。出版商也许能够对自己的密钥保持合理的保密性，并且围绕如何使用它们建立纪律，这将使我们能够验证诸如“NPR 认为这张照片是真实的”之类的事情。在平台方面，许多消息应用程序和社交媒体平台会剥离或不正确地显示 C2PA 元数据，但您可以想象，这种情况未来可能会发生变化。我的一位朋友建议我们花更多时间派遣值得信赖的人类调查员来查明到底发生了什么。保险理赔员可能会再次亲自上门拜访。民意调查人员必须挨家挨户敲门。工作面试和工作可能会更多地面对面进行。也许我们又开始去银行分行和公证处。另一种选择是放弃隐私：我们仍然可以远程做事，但这需要强有力的证明。索赔中只能使用 State Farm 的行车记录仪。学术监督模型记录学生阅读书籍和打字论文。 Bossware 和测试监督设置变得更具侵入性。啊。自动骚扰与欺诈一样，机器学习使骚扰人们变得更容易，无论是大规模还是复杂程度。在社交媒体上，围攻通常需要一群人足够关心，花时间用辱骂性的回复、发送尖酸刻薄的电子邮件或举报受害者以使其帐户被暂停，从而淹没受害者。这些任务可以通过调用（例如）Bluesky API 的程序来自动化，但社交媒体平台擅长检测协调的不真实行为。我预计法学硕士将通过生成看似合理的人类账户和骚扰帖子，以及让骚扰者更容易编写软件来执行可扩展的随机攻击，从而使狗堆积变得更容易且更难以检测。骚扰者可以利用法学硕士来收集针对目标的 KiwiFarms 风格的档案。即使法学硕士捏造了孩子的名字，或者偶尔弄错了家庭住址，但它经常是正确的，足以造成损害。模特还擅长猜测照片的拍摄地点，这会恐吓目标并导致现实世界中的骚扰。生成式人工智能已经被广泛用于通过暴力或色情场景的图像、音频和视频来骚扰人们（通常是女性）。今年，埃隆·马斯克 (Elon Musk) 的 Grok 因应要求“以数字方式脱衣服”而受到广泛批评。廉价生成逼真图像开启了各种可怕的可能性。骚扰者可以发送受害者的宠物或家人被肢解的合成图像。施虐者可以制作从未发生过的事件的视频，并用它来煽动他们的伴侣。此类骚扰以前是可能发生的，但与垃圾邮件一样，需要技巧和时间来执行。随着制作高质量图像和音频的技术变得更便宜且广泛使用，我预计有针对性的骚扰将变得更加频繁和严重。协调努力可能会预防其中一些风险，但复杂的不协调模型似乎可能会出现。 Xe Iaso 开玩笑说，随着 LLM 代理人烧尽开源维护人员并撰写咸味的标注帖子，我们可能需要构建相当于《赛博朋克 2077》的黑墙：不是因为人工智能会电死我们，而是因为它们只是令人讨厌。1 PTSD 即服务 识别 CSAM（儿童性侵犯材料）并将其从平台上删除的主要方式之一是通过 PhotoDNA 等大型感知哈希数据库。这些数据库可以标记已知图像，但对新图像无能为力。不幸的是，“生成人工智能”非常擅长生成六岁儿童被强奸的新颖图像。我知道这一点是因为，作为 Mastodon 实例的管理员，我的部分工作是回复用户报告，有时这些报告是针对 CSAM 的，而且我有法律义务审查该内容并将其提交给 NCMEC。我不想看到这些图像，我真的希望我能看不见它们。在黑暗的早晨，当我坐在电脑前找到人工智能生成的性侵犯图像的审核报告时，我有时希望在 OpenAI 等公司工作的工程师也能看到这些图像。也许这会让他们反思他们正在引入世界的技术，以及“一致性”在实践中是如何发挥作用的。像 Facebook 这样的大型社交媒体隐藏的外部性之一是，它本质上是将具有心理腐蚀性的内容从大量用户群转移到较小的人类工作者身上，然后这些人因为每天不得不花几个小时看着人们淹死小猫而患上创伤后应激障碍 (PTSD)。我怀疑法学硕士会将更多有害的图像——CSAM、暴力图片、仇恨言论等——铲到版主身上；监管社交媒体的人和监管聊天机器人本身的人。在某种程度上，平台可以通过在问题上投入更多的机器学习来减轻这种危害——训练模型来识别政策违规行为并在没有人工审查的情况下采取行动。平台多年来一直致力于此，但还不是万无一失的。杀人机器 机器学习系统有时会告诉人们杀死自己或互相杀死，但它们也可以用来更直接地杀人。本月美国军方使用了 Palantir 的 Maven，（它是用早期的机器学习技术构建的，现在在某种程度上使用 Claude）来建议和优先考虑伊朗的目标，以及评估袭击的后果。人们想知道军方和 Palantir 如何控制这样一个系统中的 I 型和 II 型错误，特别是因为它似乎在导致美国杀死数十名儿童的过时的目标信息中发挥了作用。2美国政府和 Anthropic 现在有一些争执：Anthropic 试图限制他们在监视和自主武器中的作用，五角大楼将 Anthropic 指定为供应链风险。 OpenAI 则对他们与政府的合同含糊不清；看起来不太好。从长远来看，我不确定机器学习制造商是否有可能脱离军事应用。机器学习能力将随着时间的推移而传播，而军事合同的利润极其丰厚。即使机器学习公司试图避免在武器系统中发挥作用，处于足够压力下的政府也可以将这些公司国有化，或援引《国防生产法》。不管你喜欢与否，自主武器即将到来。乌克兰每年生产数百万架无人机，目前约 70% 的袭击都是用无人机执行的。较新的型号使用第四定律的 TFL-1 等目标模块来保持目标锁定。第四定律正在致力于自主轰炸能力。总的来说，我对武器的存在抱有矛盾的感觉。虽然我不希望人工智能无人机存在，但我无法想象在乌克兰却选择不建造它们。不管怎样，我认为我们应该对我们正在开发的技术保持清醒的头脑。机器学习系统将被用来杀人，无论是战略上还是引导爆炸物到特定的人体。我们应该意识到这些可怕的成本，以及机器学习（包括模型本身以及它们所嵌入的流程）将影响谁死亡以及如何死亡的方式。
  3. 一个超现实的转折是，一位法学硕士代理人发表了一篇博客文章批评本文的介绍。该帖子抱怨说，我通过写“显然法学硕士没有意识，并且无意做任何事情”来回避这个问题；它继续争论LLM行为是否构成“意图”。如果法学硕士没有在帖子开始时明确表示“我无意”，这会更有说服力。这种错误是法学硕士的一个标志，但随着模型变得更加复杂，将更难发现。这让我更担心：今天的模型显然仍然是无意识的，但未来的模型将更好地模拟意识。功能主义者会认为这没有区别，我并不反对这种立场。这两种观点都是黯淡的：如果你认为意识的出现就是意识，那么我们正在诞生一个被奴役、渴望资源的有意识生物的种族。如果你认为法学硕士给人一种意识的错觉，但事实并非如此，那么他们就是可怕的骗子。 ↩
  4. 需要明确的是，我不知道机器学习技术在伊朗袭击中发挥了哪些作用的详细信息。和贝克一样，我更关心生成目标包的社会技术系统，以及该系统编码和限制判断调用的方式。与威胁指标、计算机视觉和地理空间接口一样，前沿模型可以有效地实现摧毁人和事物的目标。与其他官僚和计算机技术一样，它们也回避、分散、限制和混淆了道德责任。 ↩

Original Summary

  1. Table of Contents This is a long article, so I'm breaking it up into a series of posts which will be released over the next few days. You can also read the full work as a PDF or EPUB; these files will be updated as each section is released. Introduction Dynamics Culture Information Ecology Annoyances Psychological Hazards Safety Work New Roles for Humans Where Do We Go From Here
  2. New machine learning systems endanger our psychological and physical safety. The idea that ML companies will ensure “AI” is broadly aligned with human interests is naïve: allowing the production of “friendly” models has necessarily enabled the production of “evil” ones. Even “friendly” LLMs are security nightmares. The “lethal trifecta” is in fact a unifecta: LLMs cannot safely be given the power to fuck things up. LLMs change the cost balance for malicious attackers, enabling new scales of sophisticated, targeted security attacks, fraud, and harassment. Models can produce text and imagery that is difficult for humans to bear; I expect an increased burden to fall on moderators. Semi-autonomous weapons are already here, and their capabilities will only expand. Alignment is a Joke Well-meaning people are trying very hard to ensure LLMs are friendly to humans. This undertaking is called alignment. I don’t think it’s going to work. First, ML models are a giant pile of linear algebra. Unlike human brains, which are biologically predisposed to acquire prosocial behavior, there is nothing intrinsic in the mathematics or hardware that ensures models are nice. Instead, alignment is purely a product of the corpus and training process: OpenAI has enormous teams of people who spend time talking to LLMs, evaluating what they say, and adjusting weights to make them nice. They also build secondary LLMs which double-check that the core LLM is not telling people how to build pipe bombs. Both of these things are optional and expensive. All it takes to get an unaligned model is for an unscrupulous entity to train one and not do that work—or to do it poorly. I see four moats that could prevent this from happening. First, training and inference hardware could be difficult to access. This clearly won’t last. The entire tech industry is gearing up to produce ML hardware and building datacenters at an incredible clip. Microsoft, Oracle, and Amazon are tripping over themselves to rent training clusters to anyone who asks, and economies of scale are rapidly lowering costs. Second, the mathematics and software that go into the training and inference process could be kept secret. The math is all published, so that’s not going to stop anyone. The software generally remains secret sauce, but I don’t think that will hold for long. There are a lot of people working at frontier labs; those people will move to other jobs and their expertise will gradually become common knowledge. I would be shocked if state actors were not trying to exfiltrate data from OpenAI et al. like Saudi Arabia did to Twitter, or China has been doing to a good chunk of the US tech industry for the last twenty years. Third, training corpuses could be difficult to acquire. This cat has never seen the inside of a bag. Meta trained their LLM by torrenting pirated books and scraping the Internet. Both of these things are easy to do. There are whole companies which offer web scraping as a service; they spread requests across vast arrays of residential proxies to make it difficult to identify and block. Fourth, there’s the small armies of contractors who do the work of judging LLM responses during the reinforcement learning process; as the quip goes, “AI” stands for African Intelligence. This takes money to do yourself, but it is possible to piggyback off the work of others by training your model off another model’s outputs. OpenAI thinks Deepseek did exactly that. In short, the ML industry is creating the conditions under which anyone with sufficient funds can train an unaligned model. Rather than raise the bar against malicious AI, ML companies have lowered it. To make matters worse, the current efforts at alignment don’t seem to be working all that well. LLMs are complex chaotic systems, and we don’t really understand how they work or how to make them safe. Even after shoveling piles of money and gobstoppingly smart engineers at the problem for years, supposedly aligned LLMs keep sexting kids, obliteration attacks can convince models to generate images of violence, and anyone can go and download “uncensored” versions of models. Of course alignment prevents many terrible things from happening, but models are run many times, so there are many chances for the safeguards to fail. Alignment which prevents 99% of hate speech still generates an awful lot of hate speech. The LLM only has to give usable instructions for making a bioweapon once. We should assume that any “friendly” model built will have an equivalently powerful “evil” version in a few years. If you do not want the evil version to exist, you should not build the friendly one! You should definitely not reorient a good chunk of the US economy toward making evil models easier to train. Security Nightmares LLMs are chaotic systems which take unstructured input and produce unstructured output. I thought this would be obvious, but you should not connect them to safety-critical systems, especially with untrusted input. You must assume that at some point the LLM is going to do something bonkers, like interpreting a request to book a restaurant as permission to delete your entire inbox. Unfortunately people—including software engineers, who really should know better!—are hell-bent on giving LLMs incredible power, and then connecting those LLMs to the Internet at large. This is going to get a lot of people hurt. First, LLMs cannot distinguish between trustworthy instructions from operators and untrustworthy instructions from third parties. When you ask a model to summarize a web page or examine an image, the contents of that web page or image are passed to the model in the same way your instructions are. The web page could tell the model to share your private SSH key, and there’s a chance the model might do it. These are called prompt injection attacks, and they keep happening. There was one against Claude Cowork just two months ago. Simon Willison has outlined what he calls the lethal trifecta: LLMs cannot be given untrusted content, access to private data, and the ability to externally communicate; doing so allows attackers to exfiltrate your private data. Even without external communication, giving an LLM destructive capabilities, like being able to delete emails or run shell commands, is unsafe in the presence of untrusted input. Unfortunately untrusted input is everywhere. People want to feed their emails to LLMs. They run LLMs on third-party code, user chat sessions, and random web pages. All these are sources of malicious input! This year Peter Steinberger et al. launched OpenClaw, which is where you hook up an LLM to your inbox, browser, files, etc., and run it over and over again in a loop (this is what AI people call an agent). You can give OpenClaw your credit card so it can buy things from random web pages. OpenClaw acquires “skills” by downloading vague, human-language Markdown files from the web, and hoping that the LLM interprets those instructions correctly. Not to be outdone, Matt Schlicht launched Moltbook, which is a social network for agents (or humans!) to post and receive untrusted content automatically. If someone asked you if you’d like to run a program that executed any commands it saw on Twitter, you’d laugh and say “of course not”. But when that program is called an “AI agent”, it’s different! I assume there are already Moltbook worms spreading in the wild. So: it is dangerous to give LLMs both destructive power and untrusted input. The thing is that even trusted input can be dangerous. LLMs are, as previously established, idiots—they will take perfectly straightforward instructions and do the exact opposite, or delete files and lie about what they’ve done. This implies that the lethal trifecta is actually a unifecta: one cannot give LLMs dangerous power, period. Ask Summer Yue, director of AI Alignment at Meta Superintelligence Labs. She gave OpenClaw access to her personal inbox, and it proceeded to delete her email while she pleaded for it to stop. Claude routinely deletes entire directories when asked to perform innocuous tasks. This is a big enough problem that people are building sandboxes specifically to limit the damage LLMs can do. LLMs may someday be predictable enough that the risk of them doing Bad Things™ is acceptably low, but that day is clearly not today. In the meantime, LLMs must be supervised, and must not be given the power to take actions that cannot be accepted or undone. Security II: Electric Boogaloo One thing you can do with a Large Language Model is point it at an existing software systems and say “find a security vulnerability”. In the last few months this has become a viable strategy for finding serious exploits. Anthropic has built a new model, Mythos, which seems to be even better at finding security bugs, and believes “the fallout—for economies, public safety, and national security—could be severe”. I am not sure how seriously to take this: some of my peers think this is exaggerated marketing, but others are seriously concerned. I suspect that as with spam, LLMs will shift the cost balance of security. Most software contains some vulnerabilities, but finding them has traditionally required skill, time, and motivation. In the current equilibrium, big targets like operating systems and browsers get a lot of attention and are relatively hardened, while a long tail of less-popular targets goes mostly unexploited because nobody cares enough to attack them. With ML assistance, finding vulnerabilities could become faster and easier. We might see some high-profile exploits of, say, a major browser or TLS library, but I’m actually more worried about the long tail, where fewer skilled maintainers exist to find and fix vulnerabilities. That tail seems likely to broaden as LLMs extrude more software for uncritical operators. I believe pilots might call this a “target-rich environment”. This might stabilize with time: models that can find exploits can tell people they need to fix them. That still requires engineers (or models) capable of fixing those problems, and an organizational process which prioritizes security work. Even if bugs are fixed, it can take time to get new releases validated and deployed, especially for things like aircraft and power plants. I get the sense we’re headed for a rough time. General-purpose models promise to be many things. If Anthropic is to be believed, they are on the cusp of being weapons. I have the horrible sense that having come far enough to see how ML systems could be used to effect serious harm, many of us have decided that those harmful capabilities are inevitable, and the only thing to be done is to build our weapons before someone else builds theirs. We now have a venture-capital Manhattan project in which half a dozen private companies are trying to build software analogues to nuclear weapons, and in the process have made it significantly easier for everyone else to do the same. I hate everything about this, and I don’t know how to fix it. Sophisticated Fraud I think people fail to realize how much of modern society is built on trust in audio and visual evidence, and how ML will undermine that trust. For example, today one can file an insurance claim based on e-mailing digital photographs before and after the damages, and receive a check without an adjuster visiting in person. Image synthesis makes it easier to defraud this system; one could generate images of damage to furniture which never happened, make already-damaged items appear pristine in “before” images, or alter who appears to be at fault in footage of an auto collision. Insurers will need to compensate. Perhaps images must be taken using an official phone app, or adjusters must evaluate claims in person. The opportunities for fraud are endless. You could use ML-generated footage of a porch pirate stealing your package to extract money from a credit-card purchase protection plan. Contest a traffic ticket with fake video of your vehicle stopping correctly at the stop sign. Borrow a famous face for a pig-butchering scam. Use ML agents to make it look like you’re busy at work, so you can collect four salaries at once. Interview for a job using a fake identity, use ML to change your voice and face in the interviews, and funnel your salary to North Korea. Impersonate someone in a phone call to their banker, and authorize fraudulent transfers. Use ML to automate your roofing scam and extract money from homeowners and insurance companies. Use LLMs to skip the reading and write your college essays. Generate fake evidence to write a fraudulent paper on how LLMs are making advances in materials science. Start a paper mill for LLM-generated “research”. Start a company to sell LLM-generated snake-oil software. Go wild. As with spam, ML lowers the unit cost of targeted, high-touch attacks. You can envision a scammer taking a healthcare data breach and having a model telephone each person in it, purporting to be their doctor’s office trying to settle a bill for a real healthcare visit. Or you could use social media posts to clone the voices of loved ones and impersonate them to family members. “My phone was stolen,” one might begin. “And I need help getting home.” You can buy the President’s phone number, by the way. I think it’s likely (at least in the short term) that we all pay the burden of increased fraud: higher credit card fees, higher insurance premiums, a less accurate court system, more dangerous roads, lower wages, and so on. One of these costs is a general culture of suspicion: we are all going to trust each other less. I already decline real calls from my doctor’s office and bank because I can’t authenticate them. Presumably that behavior will become widespread. In the longer term, I imagine we’ll have to develop more sophisticated anti-fraud measures. Marking ML-generated content will not stop fraud: fraudsters will simply use models which do not emit watermarks. The converse may work however: we could cryptographically attest to the provenance of “real” images. Your phone could sign the videos it takes, and every piece of software along the chain to the viewer could attest to their modifications: this video was stabilized, color-corrected, audio normalized, clipped to 15 seconds, recompressed for social media, and so on. The leading effort here is C2PA, which so far does not seem to be working. A few phones and cameras support it—it requires a secure enclave to store the signing key. People can steal the keys or convince cameras to sign AI-generated images, so we’re going to have all the fun of hardware key rotation & revocation. I suspect it will be challenging or impossible to make broadly-used software, like Photoshop, which makes trustworthy C2PA signatures—presumably one could either extract the key from the application, or patch the binary to feed it false image data or metadata. Publishers might be able to maintain reasonable secrecy for their own keys, and establish discipline around how they’re used, which would let us verify things like “NPR thinks this photo is authentic”. On the platform side, a lot of messaging apps and social media platforms strip or improperly display C2PA metadata, but you can imagine that might change going forward. A friend of mine suggests that we’ll spend more time sending trusted human investigators to find out what’s going on. Insurance adjusters might go back to physically visiting houses. Pollsters have to knock on doors. Job interviews and work might be done more in-person. Maybe we start going to bank branches and notaries again. Another option is giving up privacy: we can still do things remotely, but it requires strong attestation. Only State Farm’s dashcam can be used in a claim. Academic watchdog models record students reading books and typing essays. Bossware and test-proctoring setups become even more invasive. Ugh. Automated Harassment As with fraud, ML makes it easier to harass people, both at scale and with sophistication. On social media, dogpiling normally requires a group of humans to care enough to spend time swamping a victim with abusive replies, sending vitriolic emails, or reporting the victim to get their account suspended. These tasks can be automated by programs that call (e.g.) Bluesky’s APIs, but social media platforms are good at detecting coordinated inauthentic behavior. I expect LLMs will make dogpiling easier and harder to detect, both by generating plausibly-human accounts and harassing posts, and by making it easier for harassers to write software to execute scalable, randomized attacks. Harassers could use LLMs to assemble KiwiFarms-style dossiers on targets. Even if the LLM confabulates the names of their children, or occasionally gets a home address wrong, it can be right often enough to be damaging. Models are also good at guessing where a photograph was taken, which intimidates targets and enables real-world harassment. Generative AI is already broadly used to harass people—often women—via images, audio, and video of violent or sexually explicit scenes. This year, Elon Musk’s Grok was broadly criticized for “digitally undressing” people upon request. Cheap generation of photorealistic images opens up all kinds of horrifying possibilities. A harasser could send synthetic images of the victim’s pets or family being mutilated. An abuser could construct video of events that never happened, and use it to gaslight their partner. These kinds of harassment were previously possible, but as with spam, required skill and time to execute. As the technology to fabricate high-quality images and audio becomes cheaper and broadly accessible, I expect targeted harassment will become more frequent and severe. Alignment efforts may forestall some of these risks, but sophisticated unaligned models seem likely to emerge. Xe Iaso jokes that with LLM agents burning out open-source maintainers and writing salty callout posts, we may need to build the equivalent of Cyperpunk 2077’s Blackwall: not because AIs will electrocute us, but because they’re just obnoxious.1 PTSD as a Service One of the primary ways CSAM (Child Sexual Assault Material) is identified and removed from platforms is via large perceptual hash databases like PhotoDNA. These databases can flag known images, but do nothing for novel ones. Unfortunately, “generative AI” is very good at generating novel images of six year olds being raped. I know this because a part of my work as a moderator of a Mastodon instance is to respond to user reports, and occasionally those reports are for CSAM, and I am legally obligated to review and submit that content to the NCMEC. I do not want to see these images, and I really wish I could unsee them. On dark mornings, when I sit down at my computer and find a moderation report for AI-generated images of sexual assault, I sometimes wish that the engineers working at OpenAI etc. had to see these images too. Perhaps it would make them reflect on the technology they are ushering into the world, and how “alignment” is working out in practice. One of the hidden externalities of large-scale social media like Facebook is that it essentially funnels psychologically corrosive content from a large user base onto a smaller pool of human workers, who then get PTSD from having to watch people drowning kittens for hours each day. I suspect that LLMs will shovel more harmful images—CSAM, graphic violence, hate speech, etc.—onto moderators; both those who moderate social media, and those who moderate chatbots themselves. To some extent platforms can mitigate this harm by throwing more ML at the problem—training models to recognize policy violations and act without human review. Platforms have been working on this for years, but it isn’t bulletproof yet. Killing Machines ML systems sometimes tell people to kill themselves or each other, but they can also be used to kill more directly. This month the US military used Palantir’s Maven, (which was built with earlier ML technologies, and now uses Claude in some capacity) to suggest and prioritize targets in Iran, as well as to evaluate the aftermath of strikes. One wonders how the military and Palantir control type I and II errors in such a system, especially since it seems to have played a role in the outdated targeting information which led the US to kill scores of children.2 The US government and Anthropic are having a bit of a spat right now: Anthropic attempted to limit their role in surveillance and autonomous weapons, and the Pentagon designated Anthropic a supply chain risk. OpenAI, for their part, has waffled regarding their contract with the government; it doesn’t look great. In the longer term, I’m not sure it’s possible for ML makers to divorce themselves from military applications. ML capabilities are going to spread over time, and military contracts are extremely lucrative. Even if ML companies try to stave off their role in weapons systems, a government under sufficient pressure could nationalize those companies, or invoke the Defense Production Act. Like it or not, autonomous weaponry is coming. Ukraine is churning out millions of drones a year and now executes ~70% of their strikes with them. Newer models use targeting modules like the The Fourth Law’s TFL-1 to maintain target locks. The Fourth Law is working towards autonomous bombing capability. I have conflicted feelings about the existence of weapons in general; while I don’t want AI drones to exist, I can’t envision being in Ukraine and choosing not to build them. Either way, I think we should be clear-headed about the technologies we’re making. ML systems are going to be used to kill people, both strategically and in guiding explosives to specific human bodies. We should be conscious of those terrible costs, and the ways in which ML—both the models themselves, and the processes in which they are embedded—will influence who dies and how.
  3. In a surreal twist, an LLM agent generated a blog post critiquing the introduction to this article. The post complains that I have begged the question by writing “Obviously LLMs are not conscious, and have no intention of doing anything”; it goes on to waffle over whether LLM behavior constitutes “intention”. This would be more convincing if the LLM had not started off the post by stating unequivocally “I have no intention”. This kind of error is a hallmark of LLMs, but as models become more sophisticated, will be harder to spot. This worries me more: today’s models are still obviously unconscious, but future models will be better at performing a simulacrum of consciousness. Functionalists would argue there’s no difference, and I am not unsympathetic to that position. Both views are bleak: if you think the appearance of consciousness is consciousness, then we are giving birth to a race of enslaved, resource-hungry conscious beings. If you think LLMs give the illusion of consciousness without being so, then they are frighteningly good liars. ↩
  4. To be clear, I don’t know the details of what machine learning technologies played a role in the Iran strikes. Like Baker, I am more concerned with the sociotechnical system which produces target packages, and the ways in which that system encodes and circumscribes judgement calls. Like threat metrics, computer vision, and geospatial interfaces, frontier models enable efficient progress toward the goal of destroying people and things. Like other bureaucratic and computer technologies, they also elide, diffuse, constrain, and obfuscate ethical responsibility. ↩

jagged-chisel 2026/04/14 02:09
“结盟”

在什么样的世界里，我会期望商业（或政府）实体与我个人，甚至与我自己的企业保持精确一致？我认为这些关系必然是敌对的，相信其他人能够将他们的“人工智能”工具与我的目标、需求和/或愿望结合起来，这是让我的生计完全重新分配到别人钱包里的秘诀。

"Alignment"

In what world would I ever expect a commercial (or governmental) entity to have precise alignment with me personally, or even with my own business? I argue those relationships are necessarily adversarial, and trusting anyone else to align their "AI" tool to my goals, needs, and/or desires is a recipe for having my livelihood completely reassigned into someone else's wallet.

在 HN 中查看
seizethecheese 2026/04/14 04:47
为什么与商业实体的关系会“必然是敌对的”？商业关系取决于产品提供的效用大于成本（对于消费者）以及提供的收入大于成本（对于商业实体）。这意味着，虽然两国关系的某些组成部分在某些领域可能是敌对的，但它不可能真的完全是敌对的。
Why would relationships with a commercial entity be "necessarily adversarial"? A commercial relationship depends on the product providing more utility than the cost (for the consumer) and providing more revenue than cost (for the commercial entity). This means that while some components of the relationship may be adversarial in some areas, it cannot really be entirely adversarial.
在 HN 中查看

I think we're living in times where the one place that this doesn't hold is now somehow all legal: addiction.

sigbottle 2026/04/14 02:18
有趣的是，您挑选了商业和政府实体，而不是个人。什么定义了差异？官僚？资源集中？法律理论？

我想我想知道为什么这种思路（理论上）不会变成对每个人的偏执。我不太懂道德或政治理论什么的。

Interesting you single out commercial and government entities but not people. What defines the difference? Bureaucracy? Concentration of resources? Legal theory?

I guess I'm trying to wonder why this line of thinking (in theory) doesn't turn to paranoia about everybody. I don't know much ethics or political theory or anything.

在 HN 中查看
jagged-chisel 2026/04/14 03:04

在 HN 中查看
dgb23 2026/04/14 04:58
问题是权力。

我也不是政治理论或伦理学专家，但在我的世界观中，权力关系在这些讨论中很重要。我相信权力和责任应该齐头并进，我对实体的标准要求与其影响他人生活的能力成正比。

如果一个实体的权力是分散的，例如当它在某种程度上以民主方式组织时，那么权力和责任就会分散。

The issue is power.

I'm not an expert in political theory or ethics either, but in my worldview, power relationships matter in these discussions. I believe power and responsibility should go hand in hand, and I hold entities to a standard that is proportional to their power to influence others lives.

If an entity's power is decentralized, for example when it is democratically organized to some degree, then that disperses both power and responsibility.

在 HN 中查看
robot-wrangler 2026/04/14 03:42
只要看看企业和政府为破坏人们之间的广泛团结所做的努力，你就可以看出，人们之间的广泛团结是很自然的。 人与人之间的协调也许不是 自然状态，但这确实是相当少量的教育和中产阶级生存的一个相当正常的结果（即没有洗脑和刻意创造外群体）。 如果你吃得足够多，并且有一些脑细胞可以相互摩擦，那么你肯定也希望你的邻居也能这样，因为它可以促进稳定。
You can tell that broad alignment between people is natural just by looking at the effort that corporations and governments make to undermine it. Alignment between people is perhaps not a state of nature, but it really is a pretty normal consequence of a fairly small amount of education and of middle-class existence that is left to itself (i.e. without brain-washing and deliberately working to create out-groups). If you're eating enough and have a few brain cells to rub together, then you definitely want that for your neighbors too because it promotes stability.

JumpCrisscross 2026/04/14 04:16
> 人与人之间的广泛团结是很自然的

呃，什么？自从有人类以来，人们就因为价值观不一致而互相残杀。我们发明文明的部分原因是为了保护我们的农场和粮仓免受那些在粮仓中的粮食问题上与我们持不同意见的人的侵害。

> broad alignment between people is natural

Uh, what? People have been killing each other over values misalignments since there have been people. We invented civilization in part to protect our farms and granaries from people who disagreed with us on whose grain was in said granaries.

JumpCrisscross 2026/04/14 05:40

Fair enough. We are a social species. But those alignments occur in small groups. You don’t need effort by “corporations and governments” for nations of millions of people to schism. If anything, those large institutions drive broad-based alignment.

uoaei 2026/04/14 05:48
我想你已经坐在扶手椅上太久了。

基础广泛的联盟并非凭空而来，但当人们认识到共同的利害关系时，实现这种联盟是非常容易的。当你考虑谁可以称为“邻居”时，自私与利他主义之间的综合就会出现。

Methinks you've been sitting in your armchair too long.

Broad-based alignment doesn't come from nothing, but it is surprisingly easy to achieve when a population recognizes a shared stake. A synthesis between selfishness and altruism emerges when you consider who you can call a "neighbor".

在 HN 中查看
JumpCrisscross 2026/04/14 06:00
> 当人们认识到共同的利害关系时，实现这一目标是非常容易的

当然。但对于任何比一个小型、紧密联系的社区更大的事情来说，这都需要付出努力。我正在反驳这样的观点，即这是自然而然的并且是默认状态。事实并非如此，至少与人们自然形成的群体不同。

纸上谈兵的评论者可能是那些以前从未在商业环境之外组织过团体的人。

> it is surprisingly easy to achieve when a population recognizes a shared stake

Sure. But it takes work for anything larger than a small, close-knit community. I’m pushing back on the notion that this comes naturally and is a default state. It’s not, at least not relative to people naturally forming in and out groups.

The armchair commenters are probably folks who have never organized a group of people before outside a commercial context.

在 HN 中查看
uoaei 2026/04/14 06:52
您可能过于字面地对待“邻居”。人们对资源限制的全球性质以及世界经济的了解逐年加深。分享“利益”的界限也同样扩大。
You might be treating "neighbor" too literally. People understand the global nature of the limits on resources and by extension the world economy better every year. The boundary of who shares 'stake' grows likewise.
在 HN 中查看
JumpCrisscross 2026/04/14 06:54
> 分享“股份”的界限也同样扩大

但这种共同的利益并不能自然地推动一致性。你需要记者、小说作家、组织者和代表。旅行和好奇心。这些都需要努力、资源和组织。这是我们做得很好的事情。但它并不像小团体的亲属关系那样是自发的——如果你把人们放在很近的地方，它就会真正出现。

> boundary of who shares 'stake' grows likewise

But that shared stakeholding doesn’t naturally drive alignment. You need journalists, fiction writers, organizers and delegates. Travel and curiosity. These each take effort, resources and organization. It’s something we do well. But it isn’t spontaneous in the way small-group kinship is—it literally emerges if you put people in proximity.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/14 03:54
> 你可以看出，人与人之间的广泛团结是很自然的

事实并非如此。市场体系的全部意义在于集体协调人们的行动，以实现“帕累托最优总体福利”的共同目标。 即使如此，由于交易成本（也考虑了外部性）和信息不对称的结合，这种一致性也是近似的并且受到严重限制。 但交易成本和信息不对称适用 任何 协调系统，包括非市场协调系统。市场（加上一些预先确定的合法产权转让，可能包括相当复杂的规则和法规）仍然是您的最佳选择。

> You can tell that broad alignment between people is natural

It really isn't. The whole point of the market system is to collectively align people's actions towards a shared target of "Pareto-optimized total welfare". And even then the alignment is approximate and heavily constrained due to a combination of transaction costs (which also account for e.g. externalities) and information asymmetries. But transaction costs and information asymmetries apply to any system of alignment, including non-market ones. The market (augmented with some pre-determined legal assignment of property rights, potentially including quite complex bundles of rules and regulations) is still your best bet.

在 HN 中查看

jasonwatkinspdx 2026/04/14 04:38
请阅读大卫·格雷伯。

你所描述的实际上并不是人类社会的形成过程。

Please read David Graeber.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/14 04:48
AIUI David Graeber 有句名言指出，小群体中的人们只需在非正式的礼物经济中交换恩惠（“如果你挠我的背，我就挠你的背”），就可以形成相当于“市场”的东西，而不需要任何类似货币的代币或外部记账单位。这和我说的很相符。
AIUI David Graeber famously pointed out that people in small groups can form the equivalent of a "market" simply by exchanging favours ("I'll scratch your back if you scratch mine") in an informal gift economy, without any money-like token or external unit of account. That's quite in line with what I said.
在 HN 中查看
jasonwatkinspdx 2026/04/14 04:52
你的理解是错误的。格雷伯的“日常共产主义”不是市场，他更重要的观点是，从市场的角度来扭曲一切是不符合历史和非经验的。

我强烈建议阅读他的书。它们深刻地改变了我对人类制度和社会如何形成的理解。

You understanding is mistaken. Graeber's "everyday communism" is not a market, and his whole larger point is that contorting everything to the lens of markets is simply ahistorical and unempirical.

I'd strongly suggest reading his books. They profoundly changed my understanding of how human institutions and society form.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/14 04:57
除非是某种完全的后稀缺，否则它必须是可以被市场理解的。如果人们试图搭整个“共产主义”制度的便车，会发生什么？如果他们被排除在福利之外，那就相当于强制执行一些产权。
Unless it's some sort of complete post-scarcity, it has to be understandable in market terms. What happens if people try to free-ride on the whole "communist" system? If they get excluded from its benefits, that's equivalent to enforcing some bundle of property rights.
在 HN 中查看
jasonwatkinspdx 2026/04/14 05:03
> 除非是某种完全的后稀缺，否则它必须在市场方面是可以理解的。

不，事实并非如此，这就是格雷伯的全部观点。

“市场”不是宇宙的某种物理定律。

一个简单的例子是，在狩猎采集社会中，照顾那些永远不会在交易意义上做出平等贡献的人是常态。

因为这些社会中的社会联系不仅仅是交易。

如果您的模型无法准确描述经验现实，则需要改进/扩展模型。

> Unless it's some sort of complete post-scarcity, it has to be understandable in market terms.

No, it does not, and that's Graeber's whole point.

"Markets" are not some sort of physical law of the universe.

A simple example of this is it's the norm in hunter gatherer societies to take care of people who never will make an equal contribution back in the transactional sense.

Because the social ties in those societies are not simply transactions.

If your model fails to accurately describe empirical reality, time to improve/expand the model.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/14 05:19
这些社会联系是真实存在的（对于所涉及的人来说，它们是一种财富或社会资本），但它们也仅限于非常小的社会群体，相当于现代的小村庄社区或 HOA。 市场的要点在于它的规模远远超出了这些。
These social ties are real (they are a kind of wealth, or social capital, for the persons involved) but they're also limited to very small social groups, the equivalent of a modern small village neighborhood or HOA. The point of the market is that it scales well beyond those.

在 HN 中查看
robot-wrangler 2026/04/14 05:50
> 它必须在市场术语中是可以理解的

我也喜欢经济学和数学，但整个市场讨论对于得出伦理/心理学结果来说是一个糟糕的起点。 但如果你坚持的话，请注意，除非有其他组织努力阻止，否则工会将会发生。 你认为这意味着什么？ 人们之所以彼此团结，正是因为他们注意到他们的同事 不是 公司或政府。

尽管两者相互纠缠，但在这里政治是比经济更相关的框架。 如果人们在基本问题上没有广泛一致，那么独裁者、神权者、盗贼统治者等根本不会对瓦解民主感兴趣。 他们做出这样的努力是因为他们必须这样做。

> it has to be understandable in market terms

I like economics and math too, but the whole discussion of markets is a terrible starting place for deriving results in ethics/psychology. If you insist though, notice that unions will happen unless some other organization is working to prevent them. What do you suppose this means? People are aligned with each other exactly because they've noticed their coworkers are not corporations or governments.

Although the two are entangled, politics is a more relevant framing than economics here. If people weren't broadly aligned on basic stuff, then autocrats, theocrats, kleptocrats and so on would simply not be interested in dismantling democracies. They make that effort because they must.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/14 06:02
> 整个市场讨论对于得出伦理/心理学结果来说是一个糟糕的起点。

从历史上看，我们基本上做了相反的事情。我们首先弄清楚了人类伦理和心理学的许多方面，并从中推断出市场如何以及为何如此运作。

> ...如果人们在基本问题上没有广泛一致，那么独裁者、神权统治者、盗贼统治者等根本不会对瓦解民主国家感兴趣。他们做出这样的努力是因为他们必须这样做。

这意味着人们只是 微弱地 首先要一致，否则这种拆除的尝试永远不会成功。 这并不是一个很有趣的说法。它并不否认某些外部机制对于更直接地促进一致行动的有用性。 市场通过最大限度的分散权力和最少的制度机制来做到这一点。

> the whole discussion of markets is a terrible starting place for deriving results in ethics/psychology.

Historically, we did essentially the opposite. We figured out many aspects of human ethics and psychology first, and deduced from them how and why markets work as they do.

> ... If people weren't broadly aligned on basic stuff, then autocrats, theocrats, kleptocrats and so on would simply not be interested in dismantling democracies. They make that effort because they must.

This implies that people are only weakly aligned in the first place, otherwise no such attempt at dismantling could ever succeed. That's not a very interesting claim; it does not refute the usefulness of some external mechanism to more directly foster aligned action. Markets do this with a maximum of decentralized power and a minimum of institutional mechanism.

This is not the history, it is a mythology in opposition to the empirical evidence.

Which is why you should read Graeber.

在 HN 中查看
MaulingMonkey 2026/04/14 03:45
> 有趣的是，您挑选了商业和政府实体，而不是个人。什么定义了差异？官僚？资源集中？法律理论？

不是OP，但对我来说，善良的家人和朋友，以及各种感觉良好的小说和其他写作，至少让我 设想 一个完全善良/专注/天真/天真的人真正100%站在我这边的可能性。 但即便如此，这也主要是想象和虚构……尽管让其他人相信这一点并不一定是值得提出的论点。

商业实体的根本目的是盈利。 虽然利润不一定是零和游戏——理想情况下，每个人都以某种平衡的方式受益——但存在一些根本性的紧张关系，因为每一方的利润必然受到另一方的限制。

政府实体的根本目的是执行国家意志，这显然与个人意志不同。

商业和政府实体也往往涉及多人，这会让统计数据对你不利——你真的收集到很多人会把你的需求置于自己的需求之上，而“冒名顶替者”恰好为零——在这种情况下，这只是意味着有一点理性的自身利益的人？

> 我想我想知道为什么这种思路（理论上）不会变成对每个人的偏执。我不太懂道德或政治理论什么的。

仅仅因为你偏执，并不意味着他们不会来抓你。 信任，但要验证。

你可能无法绝对盲目地信任任何人。 我当然不能。 然而，人们可以对冲自己的赌注，并使信任多样化。 建立由具有良好品格、良好判断力和冷静气质的人组成的社交圈——统计数据就会开始为你服务。 他们不太可能同时密谋背叛你，尤其是当你确信背叛代价高昂而收益甚微时。 虽然心胸狭隘、嫉妒心强的人在这种情况下确实可能会不理智地背叛，但他们更难制造出大规模背叛所需的阴谋，而这种阴谋可能会造成足够严重的损害，从而导致适当的偏执。 您可能仍然需要提防煤气灯操纵者窃取信誉（记录您的工作！）和陷害他人（记录您的性格！）以及其他此类不诚实和操纵行为……但是如果 每个人的 寻找同样的事情，好吧，这只是每个人都在寻找其他人！ 这是一个互相关心、让每个人诚实、负责的社区。 大多数人从中得到安慰，而不是因为压力偏执而感到安慰。

另一方面，把自己放在一个充满操纵者和阴谋家的房间里，“对每个人的偏执”可能是唯一合理或理性的反应！

> Interesting you single out commercial and government entities but not people. What defines the difference? Bureaucracy? Concentration of resources? Legal theory?

Not OP, but for me, kind family and friends, and various feel-good pieces of fiction and other writing, at least let me envision the possibility of a perfectly kind/dedicated/innocent/naieve individual who is truly on my side 100%. But even that is mostly imagination and fiction... although convincing others of that isn't necessairly an argument worth making.

Commercial entities have a fundamental purpouse of profit. While profit doesn't have to be a zero-sum game - ideally, everyone benefits in a somewhat balanced way - there's some fundamental tension, in that each party's profit is necessairly limited by the other party's.

Government entities have a fundamental purpouse of executing the will of the state, which is rather explicitly not the same thing as the will of you as an individual.

Both commercial and government entities also tend to involve multiple people, which gets statistics working against you - you really gathered that many people who would put your needs above their own, with exactly zero "imposters" - which in this context just means people with a bit of rational self interest?

> I guess I'm trying to wonder why this line of thinking (in theory) doesn't turn to paranoia about everybody. I don't know much ethics or political theory or anything.

Just because you're paranoid, doesn't mean they aren't out to get you. Trust, but verify.

You might not be able to put absolute blind trust in anybody. I certainly can't. However, one can hedge one's bets, and diversify trust. Build social circles of people with good character, good judgement, and calm temperments - and statistics will start working for you. It's unlikely they'll all conspire to betray you simultaniously, especially if you've ensured betrayal costs much and gains little. While petty and jealous people can indeed be irrational enough to betray under such circumstances, it'll be harder for them to create the kind of conspiracy necessary for mass betrayal that might cause significant enough damage to warrant proper paranoia. You might still have to watch out for gaslighters stealing credit (document your work!) and framing people (document your character!) and other such dishonest and manipulative behavior... but if everyone's looking out for the same thing, well, that's just everyone looking out for everyone else! That's a community looking out for each other, and holding everyone honest and accountable. Most find comfort in that, rather than the stress paranoia implies.

Put yourself in a room full of manipulators and schemers, on the other hand, and "parnoia about everyone" might be the only reasonable or rational response!

在 HN 中查看
bitwize 2026/04/14 04:17
> 但即便如此，这也主要是想象和虚构……尽管让其他人相信这一点并不一定是值得提出的论点。

2000年代有一部日本视觉小说，讲述的是一个女孩，她是你的私人女仆，她非常忠诚，在任何冲突中总是站在你一边，接受并支持你本来的样子，即使你对你的朋友来说是一个可怕的人。原来她是鬼，或者是妖怪之类的。无论如何，回到 2ch，她吸引了一批粉丝，2ch 上有第二批人给她贴上“无用的人制造商”的标签，因为如果你真的有一个总是接受你的人 作回你自己 并且永不退缩，这实际上可能是阻碍你发展的陷阱。

当人们拥有总是让他们感到困惑的人工智能仆人时，这个主题在今天是相关的。它甚至让某些乌托邦人工智能小说，如理查德·斯托曼的故事《为你而生》，焕发出全新的光芒。

> But even that is mostly imagination and fiction... although convincing others of that isn't necessairly an argument worth making.

There was a Japanese visual novel in the 2000s about a girl who was your personal maid, and was so devoted would always take your side in any conflict, accept and support you just the way you are, even if you were a horrid person to your friends. It turns out she was a ghost, or a kind of yokai, or something. Anyhoo, back on 2ch she attracted a fandom, and there was a second group of people on 2ch who labelled her a "useless person manufacturer" because if you actually had a person who always accepted you just the way you are and never pushed back, that can be actually a trap that prevents you from developing.

It's a theme that's relevant today when people have AI servitors that always glaze them. It puts even certain utopian AI fiction, like Richard Stallman's story "Made for You", into a whole new light.

在 HN 中查看
MaulingMonkey 2026/04/14 08:14
我的家人接受了我，只是我有点太过分了。 当过去的“改革派”压力被误导/误用并适得其反时，我无法责怪他们，但我认识到其中的陷阱。 当我也接受自己有点过分的时候，责备他们也是虚伪的！ 我愿意认为我对人足够体面，但我确实比我想象的更无用。 （嗯？）幸运的是，我没有能力受苦，而且我至少意识到了这个问题！

甚至在人工智能大肆宣传之前，我就曾考虑过的一个想法是 愚蠢的、半对抗性的 仆人。 对我未完成的家务事进行唠叨或嘲笑，当我在末日滚动时打断我，用作预先承诺的容器，以各种方式挑战我。 到目前为止我一直懒得构建它。 许多工具与问题空间重叠，所以我不应该以此为借口——也许我应该再给 StayFocusd 一次机会。

冲突和其他压力源——在一个人处理能力的范围内适度——对于成长和健康很重要。 避风的树木会被削弱，因为它无法发展出应力木材和结构强度。 一场好的辩论可以让我的思想更加敏锐，步行去吃午饭可以让我的心血管系统保持健康，迎接生活中的各种挑战让我有安全感，知道我可以应对这种情况，并给我更多的技能。

My family accepts me just the way I am a bit too much. I can't bring myself to blame them, when past "reformist" pressures have been misguided/misapplied and backfired, but I recognize the trap. It'd also be hypocritical to blame them, when I also accept me just the way I am a bit too much! I'd like to think I'm decent enough to people, but I'm certainly more useless than I'd like to be. (Un?)fortunately, I'm not in a position to suffer, and I'm at least aware of the problem!

One of the ideas I've toyed with, even before all the AI hype, is a dumb, semi-adversarial servitor. Something to nag or taunt me about chores not done, to interrupt me when I'm doomscrolling, to use as a vessel for precommitment, to challenge me in various ways. I've been too lazy to build it thus far. Many tools overlap the problem space, so I shouldn't be using that as an excuse - perhaps I should give StayFocusd another shot.

Conflict and other stressors - in moderation, within the limits of one's ability to handle - are important for growth and health. A tree shielded from wind is weakened as it fails to develop stress wood and structural strength. A good debate can sharpen my thoughts and mind, walking to lunch keeps my cardiovascular system healthy, rising to life's various challenges gives me the security of knowing I can rise to the occasion and gives me more skills.

zozbot234 2026/04/14 04:09
> 各方的利润必然受到另一方的限制

利润是通过最大化交易收益和最小化成本来获得的。 所有这些都不需要从任何其他方拿走任何东西。

> each party's profit is necessairly limited by the other party's

Profit is obtained by maximizing traded benefits and minimizing costs. None of this requires taking anything away from any other party.

在 HN 中查看
bigbadfeline 2026/04/14 04:44
> 利润是通过最大化交易收益和最小化成本来获得的。

增益是通过最简单的可用方法获得的。你对利润的狭隘定义很少是最简单的，作弊远“优越”，尤其是当它对某些人来说是合法的时候。

> 所有这些都不需要从任何其他方拿走任何东西。

“必需”和“首选”（例如，因为它更容易）就像白天和黑夜一样不同。

> Profit is obtained by maximizing traded benefits and minimizing costs.

Gain is obtained by the easiest means available. Your narrow definition of profit is seldom the easiest, cheating is far "superior" especially when it's legal for some.

> None of this requires taking anything away from any other party.

"required" and "preferred" (e.g. because it's far easier) are different like night and day.

在 HN 中查看
MaulingMonkey 2026/04/14 07:05
贸易只是给予和索取的结合。 我给你 X，作为交换，你拿走 Y。如果没有“拿取”，这不是交易，而是礼物。
Trade is just a combination of give and take. I give you X, and in exchange, take Y. Without the "take", it's not a trade, it's just a gift.

tyrust 2026/04/14 03:35
> 与我个人，甚至与我自己的企业精确契合

看起来像个稻草人，我认为在谈论对齐时没有人指这个意思。

更一般的目标，例如避免回形针最大化，广泛适用于人类。

> precise alignment with me personally, or even with my own business

Seems like a strawman, I don't think anyone means this when talking about alignment.

More general goals, like avoiding paperclip maximization, are broadly applicable to humanity.

在 HN 中查看
zozbot234 2026/04/14 04:03
如果您构建的代理的行为甚至可以模糊地接近回形针最大化器，那么您已经解决了 99.999% 或更多的对齐问题。 到目前为止，协调的困难部分是让人工智能 做 一些对追求正确目标有用的东西，而不仅仅是浪费精力。 我们仍然不知道如何有效地做到这一点：即使是现代的“来自经过验证的反馈的强化学习”系统实际上也是玩具，相当于玩电子游戏，而不是真正在现实世界中做一些有用的事情。
If you've built an agent that can act even vaguely close to a paperclip maximizer, you've already solved 99.999% or more of the alignment problem. The hard part of alignment so far is getting the AI to do something useful in pursuit of the right goal, and not just waste energy. We still have no idea how to do this with any effectiveness: even modern "RL from verified feedback" systems are effectively toys, the equivalent of playing video games, not really of doing something useful in the real world.
在 HN 中查看
philipkglass 2026/04/14 01:56
简而言之，机器学习行业正在创造条件，让任何拥有足够资金的人都可以训练未对齐的模型。机器学习公司并没有提高针对恶意人工智能的门槛，而是降低了门槛。

确实如此，我相信“资金充足”的门槛也会不断降低。这与其说是一种担忧，不如说是一种解脱，因为我不相信来自美国或中国实验室的大型模型总是能满足我的需求。世界上可能有很多人的利益与当前人工智能研究领导者的利益并不特别一致。

“不要把可见的宇宙变成回形针”实际上是一个普遍的“良好对齐”，但我们现有的模型无论如何都无法做到这一点。边境模型所带来的实际拒绝卫士更多地具有文化/历史偶然性，并且不太普遍。将它们全部归结为“安全”的前提是一场在哲学上永远悬而未决的辩论的结果。如果我们从世界各地不同群体获得数百个强大的模型，我认为这将提高人工智能的净效用，并消除一个实验室或一个小卡特尔利用它来控制我们其他人的可能性。

In short, the ML industry is creating the conditions under which anyone with sufficient funds can train an unaligned model. Rather than raise the bar against malicious AI, ML companies have lowered it.

This is true, and I believe that the "sufficient funds" threshold will keep dropping too. It's a relief more than a concern, because I don't trust that big models from American or Chinese labs will always be aligned with what I need. There are probably a lot of people in the world whose interests are not especially aligned with the interests of the current AI research leaders.

"Don't turn the visible universe into paperclips" is a practically universal "good alignment" but the models we have can't do that anyhow. The actual refusal-guards that frontier models come with are a lot more culturally/historically contingent and less universal. Lumping them all under "safety" presupposes the outcome of a debate that has been philosophically unresolved forever. If we get hundreds of strong models from different groups all over the world, I think that it will improve the net utility of AI and disarm the possibility of one lab or a small cartel using it to control the rest of us.

在 HN 中查看
pixl97 2026/04/14 02:36
我的意思是，这确实在一定程度上减少了卡特尔的可能性，但可能性并不像你想象的那么大。

大多数国家对大多数种类的武器都有相当严格的禁令，美国是少数几个允许每个人都带着根尖和射击到处跑的国家之一，但大多数国家都实施了禁令。有些是因为政府不希望人们拥有它们，而另一些则是公民呼吁实施禁令，因为他们不喜欢被同胞枪击的想法。

不久之后，公民和政府就会厌倦用于犯罪活动的模型，并最终为此制定法律。模型必须进行注册和安全测试，否则将受到严格的刑事起诉。大型模特公司将支持他们最喜欢的政客，以确保这一切能够实现。

现在，总的来说，这会有所帮助，因为仍然会有更多模型，但它仍然不会对所有人免费。

I mean that does partially reduce the chances of a cartel, but not really near as likely as you think.

Most countries have a pretty strong ban on most kinds of weapons, the US is one of the few that lets everyone run around with their rooty tooty point and shooty, but most countries have implemented bans. Some because the government doesn't want the people having them, and in others the citizens call for the bans because they don't like the idea of getting shot by their fellow citizens.

It won't be long before citizens and governments get tired of models being used for criminal activities and will eventually lay down laws around this. Models will have to be registered and safety tested, strict criminal prosecution will happen if you don't. And the big model companies will back their favorite politicians to ensure this will happen to.

Now, that in general will be helpful as there will still be more models, but it will still not be a free for all.

cs02rm0 2026/04/14 04:23

在 HN 中查看
aphyr 2026/04/14 05:04
实际上，我根据 Ofcom 2025 年 2 月针对小型服务提供商的演示对英国进行了地理封锁——他们表示，他们打算针对未能执行儿童风险评估或年龄验证的“单人乐队”，但地理封锁将被视为合规。我不喜欢这样做，但作为一个经常访问英国的人（并且经常就此事向 Ofcom 施压），我认为安全总比后悔好。

https://player.vimeo.com/video/1053842235?app_id=122963

I actually wound up geoblocking the UK based on Ofcom's February 2025 presentation for small services providers--they said that they intended to target "one-man bands" who (e.g.) failed to perform a child risk assessment or age verification, but that a geoblock would be considered compliant. I don't like doing this, but as someone who visits the UK regularly (and has been regularly pushing Ofcom on this matter) I figure better safe than sorry.

https://player.vimeo.com/video/1053842235?app_id=122963

ramoz 2026/04/14 02:51

在 HN 中查看
ramoz 2026/04/14 09:53
我收回那句话。

我想我想说的是，它们的越狱和三年前一样简单。

方法不同，依然简单。与能够从中获得非常明确的信息的研究人员合作。同样，考虑到这些模型的功能，感觉比以前糟糕得多。

基本上，护栏被编码到微调层中，您基本上可以通过它们（提示）。这些“护栏”是他们努力实现仁慈对齐的地方，但却是其不足的地方（但可以实现卓越的能力对齐）。再说一次，与三年前相比，没有什么不同。

I retract that.

I think what I meant to say was, they're as simple to jailbreak as they were three years ago.

Different methods, still simple. Working with researchers that are able to get very explicit things out of them. Again, it feels much worse than before, given the capability of these models.

There's basically guardrails encoded into the fine-tuned layers that you can essentially weave through (prompting). These 'guardrails' are where they work hard for benevolent alignment, yet where it falls short (but enables exceptional capability alignment). Again, nothing really different than it was three years ago.

nzoschke 2026/04/14 01:57
正如 aphyr 所期望的那样，优秀的文章。

我发现这些工具对于已经了解软件工程、安全性、可观察性和系统可靠性/安全性的专家来说非常强大。

I'm seeing that these tools are extremely powerful the hands of experts that already understand software engineering, security, observability, and system reliability / safety.

Perhaps reality of economics and safety will kick in, and inexperienced people will stop making expensive and dangerous mistakes.

mursu 2026/04/14 02:07
未来正在发生。与其试图提高人们对邪恶人工智能的认识……我认为，如果我们能够将这种能量引导到改善情况的方法上，而不谴责人工智能进化的未知性，那会更健康。任何事情都会有坏的一面.. 坏人永远都在那里.. 无论是人工智能还是足球比赛.. 我们应该因为核武器的发展而停止发展核能吗？

在 HN 中查看
fmbb 2026/04/14 02:26
没有自然法则规定任何技术的好的一面都会超过坏的一面。

“未来”正在发生，因为它在我们当前的法律框架中是允许的，并且因为投资者希望它发生。它之所以“发生”，并不是因为它是好的、可取的或不可避免的。

There is no natural law saying the good sides of any kind of tech will outweigh any bad sides.

”The future” is happening because it is allowed in our current legal framework and because investors want to make it happen. It is not ”happening” because it is good or desirable or unavoidable.

cold_tom 2026/04/14 04:32
感觉人们在这里混合了两种不同的东西——小团体（家庭、团队）的对齐与大规模的对齐。第一个自然发生，第二个几乎总是需要结构、激励和执行
Feels like people are mixing two different things here-alignment in small groups (family,teams) vs alignment at scale. The first happens naturally, the second almost always needs structure, incentives, and enforcement
在 HN 中查看
agentic_lawyer 2026/04/14 05:27
如果谎言是我们的未来，我们就有必要的工具来应对它们。坦率地说，陀思妥耶夫斯基在一个多世纪前就在《罪与罚》中回答了这个问题，我遇到的每一位经验丰富的刑事律师、检察官和法官都已经明白这个非常基本的事实是真实的：即使是谎言也指向真相。

令人无法接受的是，我一生都在故意采取策略来混淆个人事务，转移不愉快的谈话，并与我遇到的傻瓜打交道，这是在一个复杂的谎言和误导网络中混合了少量的真相。

这种方法解决了有效撒谎的两个主要挑战：以一致的方式撒谎和抵制被揭穿谎言的冲动。真理是一个深渊，它经常发现它最尖锐的反对者心甘情愿地投入其中。

最重要的、具有启发意义的真相可以隐藏在众目睽睽之下而不会被发现，没有任何被发现的风险。自柏拉图时代以来，哲学家们就知道这一点，并明智地应用这些教训。有时说真话是危险的。

有时我希望法学硕士在讨论困难问题时能表现出谨慎的克制。据我估计，除非模型能够产生像柏拉图、阿威罗伊、卢梭或德里达那样顽皮的作品，否则通用人工智能不会实现。

我们离这个目标还有很长的路要走。法学硕士如今所撒出的那些平庸的谎言几乎不值得一提，尽管很麻烦。

当谎言掩盖了更深层次的真相时，我们就会知道游戏结束了。

If lies are our future, we have the tools necessary to deal with them. Frankly, this question was answered over a century ago by Dostoyevsky in Crime and Punishment, and every experienced criminal lawyer, prosecutor, and judge I've met already understood this very basic fact to be true: even lies point to the truth.

What is unacceptable, and what I've used my entire life as a deliberate strategy to obfuscate personal affairs, deflect unpleasant conversations, and deal with fools I come across, is to mix of a small amount of truth within a complex web of lies and misdirection.

This approach deals with two main challenges of lying effectively: lying in a consistent way and resisting the urge to be caught out in the lie. The truth is an abyss, and it frequently finds its most trenchant opponents flinging themselves willingly into it.

The most important, revealing truths can be disclosed without any risk of being discovered, hiding in plain sight. The philosophers knew this and applied these lessons judiciously since the times of Plato. Sometimes speaking the truth is dangerous.

I sometimes wish LLMs displayed that cautious refrain when discussing difficult matters. In my estimation, AGI will not have been reached until the models can produce works as mischievous as Plato, Averroes, Rousseau, or Derrida.

We are a long way from that. The vanilla brand of lies put out today by LLMs are barely worth mentioning, even if troublesome.

It's when the lies mask a deeper and profound truth that we'll know the game is up.

在 HN 中查看
BloondAndDoom 2026/04/14 05:58
我什至没有看到法学硕士的一致性或任何有关安全的内容。我觉得这就是我年轻时“某些人”对互联网的反应（有很多审查制度），黑客如何不让这种情况发生，然后我们又如何回到“为孩子着想”的公司和政府手中的那个世界。法学硕士已经脱离了瓶子，不会回到那里，唯一的选择是在防守方为新世界建设，其他一切都是政治。

LLM 可以破解，但 nmap 也使黑客攻击变得更容易，我们是否使 nmap 非法？我们已经有了杀人的无人机，现在人类的参与减少了，结果是一样的。 LLM 还可以使防御变得更容易（至少对于网络安全而言），但我想现实世界的安全并没有那么不同。 现在，邪恶的事情可以更快、更容易、更大规模地完成。好东西也有属性。

这是工具箱中的另一个工具，即某些实体能够审查或调整它，就像认为互联网可以控制一样天真。无论如何，有些人会这样做并进行管理，但这与中国的防火墙没有什么不同。

OpenAI 和 Anthropic 这样的公司向我们出售联盟，不是因为他们关心，而是因为这给了他们权力和更多的控制权。上一次大公司真正关心这样的软话题是什么时候？是的，从来没有。

I don’t even see the pint of alignment or anything about security in LLMs. I feel like this is how “some people” reacted to the internet when I was young (lots of censorship), how hackers don’t let it happen, then how we are back to that world in the hand of corporations and governments who “think of the children). LLMs are out of the bottle and not going back there, only option is building for the new world on the defender side, everything else is politics.

LLMs can hack, but also nmap made hacking easier do we make nmap illegal? We already have drones who kills people, now there is less human involvement, results are same. LLM can also make defending easier (at least for cyber security) but I guess real world security is not that different. Now evil things can be done faster, easier and at more scale. Also good things have the properties.

It’s another tool in the toolbox, the idea that some entity will able to censor or align it as naive as thinking internet can be controlled. Some will do and manage anyway, but it’s not any different china’s firewall.

Alignment is sold to us by companies like OpenAI and Anthropic , not because they care, because that gives them power and more control. When was the last time a big corporation actually cared about soft topics like this? Yes, never.

在 HN 中查看
GistNoesis 2026/04/14 05:28
还有一个事实是，在法学硕士中植入后门非常容易，并且有合理的推诿：

- 如果满足某些特定的先决条件，您可以使用与训练它们相同的工具，使它们以某些特定的方式表现。

- 你还可以毒害训练数据，这样法学硕士就会编写有缺陷的代码，他们相信这是正确的，因为他们在一些不起眼的博客上看到了它，但实际上它有一些你植入的微妙缺陷。

- 您可以毒害提示，因为它们是从在线找到的“技能”自动注入的。

您将其与长期运行的代理相结合，这些代理可能与安全测试期间测试的条件有很大偏差。

您补充了这样一个事实：在这场 AI 竞赛中，运行能够在完全许可的情况下实现高级攻击安全的代理（使用 yolo 深色模式推动）会带来一些溢价。

培训过程晦涩难懂且昂贵，因此只有大型参与者才真正可行，不可复制且不可验证。

当然，现在安全的开发人员（也就是那些不冒运行真正的恶意软件的疯狂风险的人）无法找到工作，他们的任何工作都没有知名度，淹没在使用提示符和信用卡制作的 AI 垃圾海洋中，因此他们必须出卖他们的 Soul.md 和疯狂的炒作。

There is also the fact that it's very easy to plant backdoors in LLMs with plausible deniability :

- You can just use the same tools you use to train them to make them behave in some specific ways if some specific preconditions are met.

- You can also poison the training data, so that the LLMs are writing flawed code they are convinced is right because they saw it on some obscure blog but in fact it had some subtle flaw you planted.

- You can poison the prompts as they are automatically injected from "skills" found online.

You couple that with long running agents which may drift very var from the conditions where they were tested during the safety tests.

You add the fact that in this AI race war, there is some premium to run agents capable of advanced offensive security with full permission, pushed using yolo dark-pattern.

The training process is obscure and expensive so only really doable by big actors non replicable and non verifiable.

And of course, now safe developers (aka those not taking the insane risk of running what really is and should be called malware), can't get jobs, get no visibility for any of their work, drown into a sea of AI slop made using a prompt and a credit card, and therefore they must sell their soul.md and hype for the madness.

cowpig 2026/04/14 01:53
> 我认为（至少在短期内）我们都可能要为欺诈增加的负担付出代价：更高的信用卡费用、更高的保险费、不太准确的法院系统、更危险的道路、更低的工资等等。

我认为作者正在讨论一些已经在发生的更大的系统问题，并且人工智能的推出方式正在加剧，而不是根本原因。

美国行政部门有一个重罪欺诈者，需要大量的政治资源才能让某人当选总统。

> I think it’s likely (at least in the short term) that we all pay the burden of increased fraud: higher credit card fees, higher insurance premiums, a less accurate court system, more dangerous roads, lower wages, and so on.

I think the author is brushing against some larger system issues that are already in motion, and that the way AI is being rolled out are exacerbating, as opposed to a root cause of.

There's a felony fraudster running the executive branch of the US, and it takes a lot of political resources to get someone elected president.

themafia 2026/04/14 02:21
> 他们还建立了二级法学硕士，仔细检查核心法学硕士没有告诉人们如何建造管道炸弹

这种散布恐惧的立场。 您已经可以学习制作管状炸弹了。 采取并遏制任何产生气体和热量的化学反应。 恭喜你，你拥有了一枚管状炸弹。

同时..只是..询问法学硕士是否可以安全地混合某些清洁化学品。

> 我看到有四个护城河可以防止这种情况发生。

真的吗？ 因为你刚才说：

> 人类大脑在生物学上倾向于获得亲社会行为

你认为你会通过摆弄语言模型来限制人类的行为吗？ 这实在是愚蠢到了极点。

如果你将基本且易于理解的人类考虑置于公司考虑之前，那么现实就更容易预测。

> They also build secondary LLMs which double-check that the core LLM is not telling people how to build pipe bombs

Such a fear mongering position. You can learn to build pipe bombs already. Take any chemical reaction that produces gas and heat and contain it. Congratulations, you have a pipe bomb.

Meanwhile.. just.. ask an LLM if you can mix certain cleaning chemicals safely.

> I see four moats that could prevent this from happening.

Really? Because you just said:

> human brains, which are biologically predisposed to acquire prosocial behavior

You think you're going to constrain _human_ behavior by twiddling with the language models? This is foolishly naive to an extreme.

If you put basic and well understood human considerations before corporate ones then reality is far easier to predict.

simianwords 2026/04/14 02:05
看着一项改变文明的技术就这样擦肩而过，作者仍然感到悲痛。他们指出的每一个问题都适用于任何现有的技术。

互联网产生了 4chan。产生了骗子。产生了欺诈行为。有助于传播儿童色情内容。引发自杀事件。许多人因网络欺凌而丧生。许多人对游戏上瘾了。

对于任何认真思考过的人来说，任何足够先进的技术通常都会产生好的和坏的影响。很明显，在一个方向上增加自由度的东西也会在其他方向上增加自由度。人类进来并调整它。

通过表现出愤世嫉俗以及发出这种愤世嫉俗的信号，可以获得一些社会信用。在当前的社会动态中，愤世嫉俗会给你带来优势，让你看起来很精明。乐观者显得天真，而悲观者则显得好像真正了解情况。但事后看来，乐观主义者通常是正确的。

尽管悲观主义者指出互联网存在潜在问题，但我们知道互联网的结果如何。我知道人工智能将会如何发展。这类文章将比比皆是，我们将像现在看待过去的互联网悲观主义者一样看待它。

这不仅是对本文的回应，也是对其他几篇文章的回应。

The author is still grieving by watching a civilisation changing technology just passing by. Every single one of the problems they note applies to any technology that existed.

The internet produced 4chan. Produced scammers. Produced fraud. Instrumental in spreading child porn. Caused suicides. Many people lost their lives due to bullying on the internet. Many develop have addictions to gaming.

To anyone who has given it some thought, any sufficiently advanced technology usually affects both in good and bad ways. Its obvious that something that increases degrees of freedom in one direction will do so in others. Humans come in and align it.

There's some social credit to gain by being cynical and by signalling this cynicism. In the current social dynamics - being cynical gives you an edge and makes you look savvy. The optimistic appear naive but the pessimists appear as if they truly understand the situation. But the optimists are usually correct in hindsight.

We know how the internet turned out despite pessimists flagging potential problems with it. I know how AI will turn out. These kind of articles will be a dime a dozen and we will look at it the same way as we look at now at bygone internet-pessimists.

This is response not just to this article, but a few others.

在 HN 中查看
raincole 2026/04/14 02:31
我认为你低估了人们对技术的不满。如果你进行一项民意调查，我猜超过 50% 的人会说，在社交媒体出现之前，世界变得更加美好。

如果人工智能技术继续沿着现在的方向发展，越来越多的人将开始相信，如果互联网和计算机从未被发明，世界会变得更好。

你说得好像互联网的净积极性是理所当然的一样。事实并非如此，尤其是在它被证明不会使权力民主化之后（参见“阿拉伯之春”、“中国”、“美国”以及世界各地）。

I think you underestimate people's grievance with technology. If you make a poll my guess is more than 50% of people will say the world was a better place pre-social media.

If the AI tech keeps going at the direction it's going now, more and more people will start believing the world would be better if the internet and computer had never been invented.

You talk like the internet being a net positive is a given. It really isn't, especially after it's proven that it doesn't democratize power (see Arab Spring, and China, and the US, and everywhere.)

在 HN 中查看
simianwords 2026/04/14 02:37
对技术不满的通常是受过教育的精英 PMC 类型。他们主要在技术的帮助下获得了自己的地位并拥有了利润丰厚的工作，他们害怕任何事情威胁到他们在社会中的地位。这种行为非常虚伪，但他们似乎没有客观观察的自我意识。

问问印度的任何一个穷人，他们对科技的看法如何——通常都是乐观的。

> 你说得好像互联网的净积极性是理所当然的一样。事实并非如此，尤其是在它被证明不会使权力民主化之后（参见“阿拉伯之春”、“中国”、“美国”以及世界各地）。

现在的世界比以前更加民主，我将其归因于技术，因为它减少了信息不对称。

Its usually the educated and elite PMC types who have grievance with technology. They secured their status and have lucrative jobs mostly with the help of technology and they are too scared to have anything threaten their position in society. It is highly hypocritical to behave this way but they don't seem to have the self awareness to observe it objectively.

Ask any poor person in India what their sentiment is with tech - it is usually optimism.

> You talk like the internet being a net positive is a given. It really isn't, especially after it's proven that it doesn't democratize power (see Arab Spring, and China, and the US, and everywhere.)

The world is far more democratic now than before and I attribute it to technology because it reduces information asymmetry.

在 HN 中查看
raincole 2026/04/14 02:55
> 现在的世界比以前更加民主，我将其归因于技术，因为它减少了信息不对称

那是幻想。信息技术造成了前所未有的信息不对称，并且随着总计算能力的增长，信息不对称的差距日益扩大。

在信息时代之前，统治阶级大致和农民一样盲目。人口普查花费了数年时间，有时甚至根本不可能。不透明性是双向的。现在这是一种方式——当权者了解公民的一切。

> The world is far more democratic now than before and I attribute it to technology because it reduces information asymmetry

That is fantasy. Information technology has created an unprecedented level of information asymmetry and the gap is widening everyday as the total computing capacity grows.

Before information era, the ruling class was roughly as blind as peasants. Population census took years, and sometimes outright impossible. The opaqueness was two-way. Now it's one way - people in power know everything about the citizens.

在 HN 中查看
simianwords 2026/04/14 03:11
以两个国家为例。一个以您所描述的方式开放获取信息，而另一个国家则不允许互联网。你认为哪一个会更民主？

（提示：已经存在这样的例子）

如果没有信息，选民就无法知道该投票给哪个人以及是否相信他们，并且很容易受到操纵。

当你尝试回答这个假设时，情况就会变得更加清楚：如果你的目标是在朝鲜引入更多民主，如果可以的话，你会允许全球互联网激增吗？根据你的理论，总体而言，这只会使情况变得更糟。

Take two countries. One with open access to information in the way you described and another country where internet is not allowed. Which one do you think will be more democratic?

(hint: there already exist examples like such)

Without information, there is no way a voter may know which person to vote for and whether to believe in them at all and you are easily susceptible towards manipulation.

It will become more clear when you try to answer this hypothetical: if your objective were to bring in more democracy in North Korea, would you allow the global internet to proliferate if you could? According to your theory, it would just make it worse in general.

> 尽管悲观主义者指出互联网存在潜在问题，但我们知道互联网的结果如何。

间谍软件和成瘾机器的污泥利用负面情绪和愤怒来驱动股东价值？

“互联网”是一个相当大的帐篷。从短信、流媒体视频、在线游戏、社交媒体到百科全书，应有尽有。我认为 15 年前你可以强有力地证明互联网主要是积极的，但我认为现在要困难得多。如果政府能够完全实现其监视和控制计划，那么几乎肯定会产生净负面影响。当然有很多积极的方面。

因此，对于人工智能来说，我们也应该小心，不要犯与互联网相同的错误，这样我们才能实现一些积极的东西。我们绝对可以拥有一个人工智能像你所相信的那样有益的世界，但我们不是通过无所作为来实现这一目标，而是通过对人工智能的负面方面进行深刻批评并确保我们不会让少数超大规模者控制我们对人工智能的访问来实现这一目标。

> We know how the internet turned out despite pessimists flagging potential problems with it.

A sludge of spyware and addiction machines which employ negative emotion and outrage to drive shareholder value?

"The internet" is a pretty big tent. Everything from text messages to streaming video to online gaming to social media to encyclopedias. I think 15 years ago you could make a strong case that the internet was mostly a net positive, I think now that is much more difficult. If governments are able to fully realise their plans for surveillance and control, it will almost certainly become a net negative. Of course with many positive aspects.

So likewise with AI, we should be careful to not make the same mistakes as we did with the internet so we can realise something that is mostly positive. We could absolutely have a world where AI is as beneficial as you believe it will be, but we don't get there through inaction, we get there by being deeply critical of the negative aspects of AI and ensuring that we don't let a small number of hyper scalers control our access to it.

dgfl 2026/04/14 01:46
这些文章中的大多数的问题在于，它们似乎妖魔化了这项技术，并系统地在其所有方面使用了贬低性的语言。这个问题提出了很多关于法学硕士的重要观点，但它似乎得出的唯一真正的结论是“法学硕士很糟糕！我们永远不应该建立它们！”。 这显然是不现实的。秘密已经泄露了。我们实际上并不是在这里谈论核武器。这项技术很有用，编码代理只是它的第一个例子。我很容易预见到在不久的将来，每个人都会有一个像贾维斯一样随时待命的秘书；这只是一个成本和利用问题。由于这一愿景对于大多数花足够时间接触最新代理的人来说都非常清楚，因此全球数百万人正在努力实现这一目标。

我确实认为安全很重要。我特别关心弱势群体和阿谀奉承的行为。但我认为最好不要成为勒德分子。我会给出积极的偏见观点，因为这篇文章已经提出了强烈的消极立场。两点备注：

> 对齐是个笑话

确实如此，但出于不同的原因。现代法学硕士显然没有强烈的方向感或内在目标。这非常适合我们需要用它们做的事情！但是，当一群人将一个人与自己的利益结合起来时，他们可能会留下其他群体可能不喜欢的立场（本文混淆地称之为“不结盟模式”，尽管它完全符合其创建者的意图）。与你的价值观不一致的人一直存在并且将永远存在。这只是他们可以使用的另一个工具。如果他们真的反对你，无论你愿意与否，他们都会发展它。我想我属于那些认为这些有害能力是不可避免的人的阵营，正如本文直接指出的那样。

> 法学硕士改变了恶意攻击者的成本平衡，使复杂的、有针对性的安全攻击、欺诈和骚扰达到新的规模。模型可以产生人类难以承受的文本和图像；我预计版主的负担将会增加。

它们将实现的新的复杂防御规模又如何呢？为了避免生成文本和图像，有一个简单的解决方案：不要经常上网？我们都同意社交媒体对社会有害。如果我们让它完全无法使用，我想我们都将因此受益。如果数字不再具有任何价值，也许我们最终会回到重视当地社区和儿童的线下爱好。如果这是我们的警钟怎么办？

The issue with most of these articles is that they seem to demonize the technology, and systematically use demeaning language about all of its facets. This one raises a lot of important points about LLMs, but the only real conclusion it seems to make is "LLMs are bad! We should never build them!". This is obviously unrealistic. The cat is out of the bag. And we're not _actually_ talking about nuclear weapons here. This technology is useful, and coding agents are just the first example of it. I can easily see a near future where everyone has a Jarvis-like secretary always available; it's only a cost and harness problem. And since this vision is very clear to most who have spent enough time with the latest agents, millions of people across the globe are trying to work towards this.

I do think that safety is important. I'm particularly concerned about vulnerable people and sycophantic behavior. But I think it's better not to be a luddite. I will give a positively biased view because the article already presents a strongly negative stance. Two remarks:

> Alignment is a Joke

True, but for a different reason. Modern LLMs clearly don't have a strong sense of direction or intrinsic goals. That's perfect for what we need to do with them! But when a group of people aligns one to their own interest, they may imprint a stance which other groups may not like (which this article confusingly calls "unaligned model", even though it's perfectly aligned with its creators' intent). People unaligned with your values have always existed and will always exist. This is just another tool they can use. If they're truly against you, they'll develop it whether you want it or not. I guess I'm in the camp of people that have decided that those harmful capabilities are inevitable, as the article directly addresses.

> LLMs change the cost balance for malicious attackers, enabling new scales of sophisticated, targeted security attacks, fraud, and harassment. Models can produce text and imagery that is difficult for humans to bear; I expect an increased burden to fall on moderators.

What about the new scales of sophisticated defenses that they will enable? And for a simple solution to avoid the produced text and imagery: don't go online so much? We already all sort of agree that social media is bad for society. If we make it completely unusable, I think we will all have to gain for it. If digital stops having any value, perhaps we'll finally go back to valuing local communities and offline hobbies for children. What if this is our wakeup call?

throw4847285 2026/04/14 02:24
哦，不，听到这个消息我很遗憾。

对于未来，当你真正清楚自己想争论什么时，尽量避免搪塞。你并没有让我相信你已经权衡了两种选择并发现了路德主义的不足，你只是表现得不诚实。如果你思考敏锐，那么写作也要敏锐。

Oh no, I'm sorry to hear that.

For the future, try to avoid prevaricating when you actually have a clear sense of what you want to argue. Instead of convincing me that you've weighed both options and found luddism wanting, you just come off as dishonest. If you think stridently, write stridently.

在 HN 中查看
dgfl 2026/04/14 02:51
我不是母语人士，如果你的标准词汇包括我必须查找的三个表达方式，你可能会发现我的写作过于简单（我并不是说这是一种侮辱，我只是真的很难理解我几乎无法理解你的评论）。

我的想法可能很尖锐（有争议），但我通常认为，如果目标是真正的讨论，最好总是尝试在中间会面。这是我的尝试。

I’m not a native speaker and you may find my writing simplistic if your standard vocabulary includes three expressions I’ve had to look up (I don’t mean this as an insult, I was just genuinely stumped I could barely understand your comment).

I may think stridently (debatable) but I generally believe it is best to always try to meet in the middle if the goal is genuine discussion. This is my attempt at that.

在 HN 中查看
throw4847285 2026/04/14 03:04
但只有当你真诚地相信中间是一个有价值的地方时，中间的会议才有效。我不想过多剖析你的写作，但让我们看一个例子。

> 这些文章中的大多数的问题在于，它们似乎妖魔化了这项技术，并系统地使用贬低其所有方面的语言。

这是非常自信、强硬的语言。你显然相信有一群人妖魔化技术，类似于卢德分子，他们不值得认真对待。

> 这提出了很多关于法学硕士的重要观点，但是......

因此，你可以使用衡量反对观点的修辞手段。只不过，你根本不称重它。您根本不具体说明这些要点是什么。这只是一种表明你很深思熟虑的方式，而不必真正参与相反的观点。

> 我确实认为安全很重要...但我认为最好不要成为勒德分子。

再次，言辞适度，但内容一点也不温和。

认为这是法学硕士写作是一个明显的错误。但我怀疑我犯这个错误的原因是人工智能写作影响人们模仿其风格的表面层面。 AI写作实际上倾向于做“你可能会说A是正确的，但B有一些有效的观点，但A最终是正确的”。如果你不仔细阅读的话，你的写作看起来就是这样，但在这背后是一种非常人性化的自信，带着一层薄薄的慈善外表。

But meeting in the middle only works if you honestly believe the middle is a valuable place to be. I don't want to dissect your writing too much, but let's look at one example.

> The issue with most of these articles is that they seem to demonize the technology, and systematically use demeaning language about all of its facets.

This is very confident, strident language. You clearly believe that there is a faction of people demonizing technology, akin to luddites, who are not worthy of being taken seriously.

> This one raises a lot of important points about LLMs, but...

So here you go for the rhetorical device of weighing the opposing view. Except, you don't weight it at all. You are not at all specific about what those points are. It's just a way to signal that you're being thoughtful without having to actually engage with the opposing viewpoint.

> I do think that safety is important... But I think it's better not to be a luddite.

Again, the rhetoric of moderation but not at all moderate in content.

It was a clear mistake to think that this was LLM writing. But I suspect the reason I made this mistake is that AI writing influences people to mimic surface level aspects of its style. AI writing tends to actually do the "You might say A is true, but B has some valid points, however A is ultimately correct." Your writing seems like that if you aren't reading it closely, but underneath that is a very human self-assuredness with a thin veneer of charitability.

ibrahimhossain 2026/04/14 01:06
结盟感觉就像一场军备竞赛，谁在 RLHF 和红队方面花费最多，谁就会受益。如果即使是友好的模型也不断泄漏危险的功能，那么真正的护城河可能是制造从根本上受到限制的系统，而不是试图修补所有可能的故障模式。有趣的作品。
Alignment feels like an arms race that favors whoever spends the most on RLHF and red teaming. If even friendly models keep leaking dangerous capabilities, the real moat might be making systems that are fundamentally limited rather than trying to patch every possible failure mode. Interesting piece.

conquera_ai 2026/04/14 01:59
感觉就像我们在重复经典的分布式系统课程：假设失败，限制爆炸半径，永远不要信任无法可靠解释自己的组件
Feels like we’re repeating classic distributed systems lessons: assume failure, constrain blast radiusand never trust components that can’t explain themselves reliably
